bytes.topic: Database Selection — OLTP vs OLAP, relational vs document vs wide-column vs search
bytes.title: PostgreSQL — OLTP, OLAP, and Four Paradigms in One Database
bytes.body:
PostgreSQL's hybrid nature makes it tempting to use for everything—but workload type still matters. For OLTP, its row-store, MVCC, and strict ACID semantics shine: high-concurrency writes with predictable sub-millisecond latency. For OLAP at scale, it struggles without extensions—Citus adds columnar storage via `CREATE TABLE ... USING columnar`, and `pg_partman` manages time-series partitioning to prune scan ranges with BRIN indexes.

PostgreSQL natively spans three paradigms: relational (default heap tables), document (JSONB + GIN indexes with `jsonb_path_ops`), and full-text search (`tsvector`/`tsquery` + `pg_trgm` for trigram similarity). Wide-column is the weak spot—no native sparse columns, and simulating it with JSONB adds write amplification on updates.

Audit access patterns before adding a specialist store: point lookups + transactions → normalized relational tables with B-tree indexes; semi-structured event data → JSONB with `@>` containment queries; keyword search → `tsvector` with `plainto_tsquery`; analytics at scale → Citus columnar or an external OLAP. PostgreSQL's extensibility delays polyglot pressure, but not indefinitely.

bytes.topic: PostgreSQL — ACID transactions, indexes, JSONB, partitioning, extensions (pgvector)
bytes.title: PostgreSQL — GIN Indexes on JSONB: End the Seq Scans
bytes.body:
JSONB columns without a GIN index force PostgreSQL to deserialize and scan every row — a Seq Scan that kills performance at scale. A standard GIN index covers all keys and values, enabling the @>, ?, ?|, and ?& operators to run as index lookups instead.

For narrower, read-heavy access patterns, jsonb_path_ops cuts index size significantly by only covering containment paths. The tradeoff: key-existence checks (? operator) no longer use the index.

Always verify with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS). A Bitmap Heap Scan via the GIN index is the happy path. If you're still hitting a Seq Scan, check whether your WHERE clause predicate is actually covered by the chosen operator class.

GIN indexes carry higher write amplification than B-tree. On insert-heavy workloads, fastupdate=on batches pending entries in a side list — tune gin_pending_list_limit (default 4MB) to control when that list flushes. Setting fastupdate=off gives consistent write latency at the cost of slower individual inserts.

bytes.topic: Cassandra — partition key design, wide rows, eventual consistency, data modeling for queries
bytes.title: postgresql_de → Cassandra: Partition Keys, Wide Rows, and Query-First Modeling
bytes.body:
Coming from PostgreSQL, Cassandra's data model is a paradigm shift. In PostgreSQL, you normalize data and let the query planner handle access. In Cassandra, you model tables around access patterns — no joins, no ad-hoc queries.

The partition key determines which node stores your data. A poorly chosen key creates hotspots — one node overwhelmed while others idle. Unlike PostgreSQL's declarative table partitioning (transparent to the query planner), Cassandra's partition key is a hard routing decision baked into the schema.

Wide rows store thousands of clustered columns per partition — a sorted map within a row. Use CLUSTERING ORDER BY to pre-sort data, eliminating runtime ORDER BY entirely.

Cassandra uses eventual consistency by default. CONSISTENCY QUORUM requires a majority of replicas to agree on reads and writes — stronger guarantees at throughput cost. PostgreSQL's MVCC gives you serializable isolation for free; in Cassandra, you design around conflict-free data or use lightweight transactions (IF NOT EXISTS) sparingly, since they carry a Paxos round-trip penalty.

Denormalize aggressively: duplicate data across tables to serve each query pattern from a single partition scan.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Cassandra CQL: time-series events partitioned by user
CREATE TABLE user_events (
  user_id   UUID,
  event_time TIMESTAMP,
  event_type TEXT,
  payload    TEXT,
  PRIMARY KEY (user_id, event_time)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (event_time DESC)
  AND default_time_to_live = 2592000;

-- Quorum read: majority of replicas must agree
SELECT * FROM user_events
WHERE user_id = ? AND event_time > ?
USING CONSISTENCY QUORUM;

bytes.topic: DynamoDB — single table design, partition key + sort key, GSI/LSI, capacity modes
bytes.title: postgresql_de Meets DynamoDB — Mapping Single-Table Design, GSI/LSI, and Capacity Modes
bytes.body:
DynamoDB's single-table design collapses all entity types into one table, encoding relationships directly into composite `pk + sk` key strings — e.g. `USER#123` / `ORDER#456`. From a postgresql_de perspective, this is deliberately anti-normalized: what you'd split across `users`, `orders`, and a foreign key, DynamoDB merges into one row with a JSONB-like blob.

The `pk` (partition key) maps conceptually to the leading column of a PostgreSQL composite primary key; `sk` (sort key) is the second. GSIs (Global Secondary Indexes) let DynamoDB query non-PK attributes — the equivalent of `CREATE INDEX` on arbitrary columns. LSIs (Local Secondary Indexes) keep the same partition key but sort on a different attribute — analogous to `CREATE INDEX ON entities (pk, data->>'amount')`.

Capacity modes (provisioned RCU/WCU vs. on-demand) have no postgresql_de equivalent. PostgreSQL scales through PgBouncer connection pooling, `max_connections`, autovacuum tuning, and read replicas — throughput is managed at the infrastructure layer, not the table level.

When mirroring DynamoDB access patterns in postgresql_de, a polymorphic `entities` table with a `JSONB data` column plus targeted GIN/B-tree indexes delivers comparable query flexibility while retaining JOIN capability.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Mirroring DynamoDB single-table design in postgresql_de
CREATE TABLE entities (
  pk          TEXT        NOT NULL,  -- e.g. USER#123
  sk          TEXT        NOT NULL,  -- e.g. ORDER#456
  entity_type TEXT        NOT NULL,
  data        JSONB,
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(),
  PRIMARY KEY (pk, sk)
);

-- GSI equivalent: query across all entity types by date
CREATE INDEX idx_entities_type_created
  ON entities (entity_type, created_at DESC);

-- LSI equivalent: same PK, alternate sort on a data attribute
CREATE INDEX idx_entities_pk_amount
  ON entities (pk, ((data->>'amount')::numeric) DESC);

bytes.topic: Elasticsearch — inverted index, mapping, analyzers, query DSL, aggregations
bytes.title: postgresql_de Users: Elasticsearch Core Concepts Mapped to What You Already Know
bytes.body:
PostgreSQL's `tsvector` and GIN indexes are your mental model for Elasticsearch's inverted index — both tokenize text and map terms to document IDs. Elasticsearch does this at write time, distributed across shards, not per-column.

**Mapping ≈ DDL:** Define field types explicitly — `keyword` (exact match, no tokenization) vs `text` (analyzed). Dynamic mapping in production is as dangerous as `CREATE TABLE` with unknown types. Lock it down upfront.

**Analyzers ≈ `to_tsvector(config, text)`:** The `standard` analyzer maps to `pg_catalog.english`. Custom analyzers chain character filters → tokenizer → token filters — identical in concept to PostgreSQL's `CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION`.

**Query DSL ≈ SQL:** `term` = `WHERE col = 'val'`. `match` = `WHERE col @@ to_tsquery(...)`. `bool` with `must/should/must_not` = `AND/OR/NOT`. `range` = `BETWEEN`.

**Aggregations ≈ GROUP BY:** `terms` agg = `GROUP BY col LIMIT n`. `date_histogram` = `GROUP BY date_trunc('day', col)`. Unlike PostgreSQL, aggs execute in-memory per shard then merge — unbounded `size` will OOM. Respect `search.max_buckets`.

bytes.topic: ClickHouse — columnar storage, materialized views, MergeTree engine, OLAP queries
bytes.title: postgresql_de → ClickHouse — MergeTree, Columnar Storage, and OLAP Without the Row-Fetch Tax
bytes.body:
PostgreSQL's heap storage fetches entire rows even for single-column aggregates—reading unnecessary bytes on every analytical query. ClickHouse's columnar layout reads only referenced columns, making COUNT(*) over 1B rows near-instant.

The MergeTree engine is ClickHouse's workhorse. Unlike PostgreSQL's B-tree, MergeTree uses a sparse primary index: one entry per 8,192-row granule. Data is physically sorted by the ORDER BY key at write time—analogous to a PostgreSQL clustered index, but coarser-grained and append-only. Placing a high-cardinality column first (like user_id) kills compression; prefer low-cardinality first (action, then user_id).

Materialized views behave nothing like PostgreSQL's REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW. In ClickHouse, MVs are incremental INSERT triggers—rows flow into the MV automatically on every write. Pair with SummingMergeTree or AggregatingMergeTree for real-time pre-aggregation at zero maintenance cost.

Key migration traps for postgresql_de developers: avoid frequent UPDATE/DELETE (triggers expensive part merges); partition by toYYYYMM(event_date) for time-series data; replace COUNT(DISTINCT id) with uniqCombined64() for HyperLogLog-backed approximate counts.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- postgresql_de: row heap + B-tree index
-- ClickHouse equivalent: columnar MergeTree + sparse index

CREATE TABLE events (
    event_date  Date,
    user_id     UInt64,
    action      LowCardinality(String),
    duration_ms UInt32
) ENGINE = MergeTree()
PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(event_date)
ORDER BY (action, user_id);

-- Incremental MV: fires on INSERT, no REFRESH needed
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW events_daily_agg
ENGINE = SummingMergeTree()
ORDER BY (event_date, action) AS
SELECT event_date, action,
       sum(duration_ms) AS total_ms,
       count()          AS hits
FROM events
GROUP BY event_date, action;

bytes.topic: TimescaleDB — hypertables, continuous aggregates, compression, time-series queries
bytes.title: PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB — Hypertables, Continuous Aggregates, and Compression in Practice
bytes.body:
TimescaleDB extends PostgreSQL with time-series primitives that slot directly into your existing psql workflow. A hypertable is a standard PostgreSQL table partitioned automatically by time — create it with `SELECT create_hypertable('metrics', 'recorded_at')` after your normal `CREATE TABLE`. Queries use identical SQL; TimescaleDB's planner prunes irrelevant chunks automatically.

Continuous aggregates replace expensive recurring rollup jobs. Define a materialized view with `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW hourly_avg WITH (timescaledb.continuous) AS SELECT time_bucket('1 hour', recorded_at) AS bucket, avg(value) FROM metrics GROUP BY bucket`. Refresh policy handles incremental updates — no full recompute.

Native compression is column-oriented: `ALTER TABLE metrics SET (timescaledb.compress, timescaledb.compress_orderby = 'recorded_at DESC'); SELECT add_compression_policy('metrics', INTERVAL '7 days')`. Older chunks compress automatically, often achieving 90%+ size reduction while remaining queryable via transparent decompression. All of this runs inside your existing PostgreSQL connection — no separate daemon, no new driver.

bytes.topic: Indexing Strategy — B-tree vs hash vs GIN vs BRIN, covering indexes, partial indexes
bytes.title: PostgreSQL Indexing Strategy — B-tree, Hash, GIN, BRIN, Covering, and Partial Indexes
bytes.body: PostgreSQL defaults to B-tree for a reason: it handles equality, range, ORDER BY, and IS NULL efficiently on most scalar types. Hash indexes are faster for pure equality lookups (=) but useless for ranges and can't be WAL-logged before PG10 — prefer them only on high-cardinality UUID or hash-keyed columns. GIN shines on composite values: use it for jsonb @>, array &&, and tsvector @@ queries. BRIN is the secret weapon for append-only, physically ordered tables (logs, time-series) — a BRIN on created_at across 100M rows costs kilobytes of storage versus a B-tree's gigabytes, at the cost of less precision. Covering indexes (INCLUDE) let you satisfy queries entirely from the index without a heap fetch — critical for hot read paths. Partial indexes slash index size and maintenance cost when your WHERE clause carves out a selective subset. Stack them: a partial GIN on a jsonb column filtered to active rows only is a valid production pattern in PostgreSQL.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Covering index: avoid heap fetch on hot user lookup
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email_covering
  ON users (email)
  INCLUDE (id, display_name);

-- Partial index: only index unprocessed jobs (selective subset)
CREATE INDEX idx_jobs_pending
  ON jobs (created_at)
  WHERE status = 'pending';

-- GIN index for JSONB containment queries
CREATE INDEX idx_events_payload_gin
  ON events USING gin (payload jsonb_path_ops);

-- BRIN for append-only time-series (low storage cost)
CREATE INDEX idx_logs_ts_brin
  ON logs USING brin (logged_at) WITH (pages_per_range = 128);

-- Verify index usage
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT id, display_name FROM users WHERE email = 'sravan@example.com';

bytes.topic: Query Optimization — EXPLAIN ANALYZE, slow query log, N+1, query planning
bytes.title: PostgreSQL Query Optimization — EXPLAIN ANALYZE, Slow Query Log, and Killing N+1
bytes.body:
EXPLAIN ANALYZE is your ground truth in PostgreSQL — unlike EXPLAIN alone, it executes the query and surfaces actual row counts and timing per plan node. Run it as `EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)` to expose I/O alongside timing. Watch for nodes where `actual rows` vastly exceeds `rows` estimate: this is the planner operating on stale statistics. Fix it with `ANALYZE <table>` or tune `autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor` for high-churn tables.

A `Seq Scan` on a large table with low-selectivity filters almost always means a missing index. A `Nested Loop` with escalating loop counts is the mechanical fingerprint of N+1 — dozens of identical parameterized queries hitting the same table. Collapse them with `= ANY($1::int[])`, a lateral join, or a CTE.

For fleet-wide visibility, pair `pg_stat_statements` with the slow query log (`log_min_duration_statement`). Sort by `mean_exec_time` to find chronic offenders. When debugging planner choices, toggle join strategies with `SET enable_hashjoin = off` locally — but revert before committing. The planner's cost model is tunable via `seq_page_cost` and `random_page_cost`; dropping `random_page_cost` to 1.1 on SSD-backed instances often unlocks better index usage.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Enable fleet-wide query telemetry
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_stat_statements;

-- Surface the worst offenders by mean latency
SELECT
  left(query, 80)        AS query_preview,
  calls,
  round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS mean_ms,
  round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Deep-dive: actual vs. estimated rows + buffer hits
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT u.id, COUNT(p.id)
FROM users u
JOIN posts p ON p.user_id = u.id
WHERE u.created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
GROUP BY u.id;

bytes.topic: Data Modeling — normalization vs denormalization, schema design for access patterns
bytes.title: PostgreSQL Data Modeling — Normalization vs. Denormalization for Real Access Patterns
bytes.body:
Normalized schemas protect integrity via FK constraints and reduce write amplification — the right default when writes dominate. Denormalization trades storage for read throughput, and PostgreSQL gives you surgical tools for this tradeoff without abandoning relational guarantees.

For read-heavy access patterns, `MATERIALIZED VIEW` with `REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY` pre-computes expensive joins without blocking live reads. For sparse or semi-structured attributes, a `JSONB` column with a `GIN` index (`jsonb_path_ops`) avoids wide nullable tables while keeping query flexibility via `@>` and `#>>` operators.

Use `GENERATED ALWAYS AS (expr) STORED` for derived columns that stay consistent automatically — no application-side sync needed. When queries filter on a predictable subset, partial indexes (`WHERE status = 'active'`) compress index size and sharpen planner selectivity.

Diagnose your real access patterns with `pg_stat_user_tables`: high `seq_scan` counts on large tables signal under-indexing or over-normalization. Covering indexes (`INCLUDE (col)`) eliminate heap fetches on hot read paths — effectively giving you denormalization benefits inside an otherwise normalized schema.

bytes.topic: Transactions & ACID — isolation levels, optimistic vs pessimistic locking
bytes.title: PostgreSQL Transactions — Isolation Levels and Optimistic vs Pessimistic Locking
bytes.body:
PostgreSQL's default isolation level is READ COMMITTED — each statement gets a fresh snapshot, so two statements within the same transaction can observe different data if a concurrent commit lands between them. For consistent reads across statements, upgrade to REPEATABLE READ or SERIALIZABLE.

PostgreSQL uses MVCC under the hood: reads never block writes, but write-write conflicts still cause waits or errors.

Pessimistic locking with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE acquires row-level locks immediately, blocking concurrent writers until your transaction commits. Add SKIP LOCKED for queue-style workloads — it skips already-locked rows instead of waiting, enabling parallel consumers.

Optimistic locking avoids database locks entirely. Add a version INTEGER column, increment it on every write, and check it in the WHERE clause. If UPDATE returns 0 rows affected, a concurrent transaction won the race — retry at the application layer.

SERIALIZABLE in PostgreSQL uses Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI): it detects read-write dependency cycles at commit time and raises ERROR 40001 (serialization failure) rather than silently returning stale data. Safest option, but mandates explicit retry logic. For high-contention rows like counters or seat reservations, pessimistic locking under REPEATABLE READ usually outperforms optimistic strategies by eliminating retry stampedes.

bytes.topic: Replication — leader/follower, multi-leader, leaderless, consistency trade-offs
bytes.title: PostgreSQL Replication — The synchronous_commit Dial and What Each Mode Actually Guarantees
bytes.body:
PostgreSQL's streaming replication ships WAL segments from primary to standbys continuously. The consistency knob is `synchronous_commit`, with five levels: `off` (acknowledge before local flush — up to `wal_writer_delay` of data loss risk), `local` (flush locally, replicate async), `remote_write` (standby OS has received WAL), `remote_apply` (standby has applied — read-your-writes safe on hot standbys), and `on` (alias for `remote_write` in physical replication).

Replication slots pin WAL on the primary until each consumer catches up. A lagging slot fills `pg_wal` and can crash the primary — set `max_slot_wal_keep_size` as a hard cap and monitor `pg_replication_slots.confirmed_flush_lsn` vs `pg_current_wal_lsn()` for drift.

For lag-aware read routing, query `pg_stat_replication.replay_lag` on the primary before deciding whether to serve from a hot standby or fall back to primary.

Multi-leader isn't native PostgreSQL — EDB's BDR (built on pglogical) handles it with configurable `bdr.conflict_resolution` policies. Leaderless quorum writes don't exist in core PostgreSQL; Citus distributes shards but still routes through a coordinator.

`synchronous_standby_names = 'FIRST 1 (replica1, replica2)'` lets you require only a quorum subset to acknowledge, balancing availability against durability.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Inspect lag and sync state from the primary
SELECT
  application_name,
  sync_state,
  write_lag,
  flush_lag,
  replay_lag,
  pg_wal_lsn_diff(sent_lsn, replay_lsn) AS bytes_behind
FROM pg_stat_replication;

-- Elevate durability for a single critical transaction
SET LOCAL synchronous_commit = remote_apply;
INSERT INTO audit_log (event, actor) VALUES ('payment_confirmed', 'usr_42');
COMMIT; -- blocks until standby has replayed, not just received

bytes.topic: Sharding — horizontal partitioning, shard key selection, hot spots
bytes.title: PostgreSQL Sharding — Shard Key Selection and Avoiding Hot Spots with Citus
bytes.body:
PostgreSQL alone doesn't shard — you need the `citus` extension to distribute tables across worker nodes. The shard key is the most consequential decision: it determines how rows are hashed across shards and which queries get pruned to a single node vs. broadcast to all.

`create_distributed_table('orders', 'user_id')` hashes `user_id` across 32 shards by default. This works because `user_id` has high cardinality and almost every query filters on it — Citus prunes to a single shard without touching others. Never use `created_at` or a serial `id` as the shard key: sequential values cluster in early shards during initial load, and range queries scatter across all nodes, turning into expensive broadcast queries.

Hot spots appear when a few shard key values carry disproportionate write volume — a single power-user account generating 80% of inserts, for example. Diagnose with `citus_stat_statements` grouped by `shardid`. Structural skew requires a composite key like `(tenant_id, user_id)` to redistribute load; incidental skew can be fixed with `rebalance_table_shards()`.

Always colocate related tables on the same shard key so JOINs stay node-local and don't require cross-node data shuffles.

bytes.topic: Backup & Recovery — point-in-time recovery, backup strategies, RTO vs RPO
bytes.title: PostgreSQL PITR — Backup Strategies That Actually Hit Your RTO and RPO
bytes.body:
PostgreSQL's Point-in-Time Recovery relies on a base backup plus a continuous WAL (Write-Ahead Log) archive. Your RPO is directly tied to your WAL archiving frequency — `archive_command` ships WAL segments, so a 5-minute segment flush means up to 5 minutes of data loss on failure. Use `archive_status` and monitor `pg_stat_archiver.last_failed_time` to catch silent archive failures before they wreck your recovery window.

For RTO, the bottleneck isn't restoring the base backup — it's WAL replay. A 100GB database with 12 hours of WAL can take 30–90 minutes to replay. Use `pg_basebackup` with `-Ft -z` for compressed tar-format base backups, and set `recovery_target_time` in `postgresql.conf` to stop replay at a precise timestamp. Combine with `restore_command` pointing to your WAL archive (S3, Azure Blob, or `pgBackRest`). `pgBackRest` is the production standard: it supports parallel WAL fetch, delta restores, and retention policies out of the box — critical when base backups exceed 500GB.
bytes.code_snippet:
# postgresql.conf — WAL archiving setup
archive_mode = on
archive_command = 'pgbackrest --stanza=main archive-push %p'
wal_level = replica

# recovery.conf (or postgresql.conf in PG12+)
restore_command = 'pgbackrest --stanza=main archive-get %f "%p"'
recovery_target_time = '2026-04-15 14:30:00+00'
recovery_target_action = 'promote'

-- Check archive health
SELECT last_archived_wal, last_failed_wal, last_failed_time
FROM pg_stat_archiver;

bytes.topic: Connection Pooling — PgBouncer, connection limits, pool sizing
bytes.title: PostgreSQL Connection Pooling — Sizing PgBouncer Before max_connections Kills You
bytes.body:
Every PostgreSQL backend process costs ~5–10 MB of RAM. With the default max_connections = 100, a spike of 150 app threads will queue or outright reject connections. PgBouncer solves this by multiplexing thousands of client connections onto a small PostgreSQL-side pool.

Use transaction pooling (pool_mode = transaction) for stateless web workloads — it recycles a server connection the moment a transaction commits. Avoid session pooling unless your app uses SET LOCAL, advisory locks, or prepared statements, all of which are connection-scoped.

Pool sizing formula: default_pool_size ≈ (num_cores × 2) + effective_spindle_count. For a 4-vCPU Azure Flexible Server, that's ~10 server connections per database. Set max_client_conn to your total app thread ceiling (e.g. 500).

Monitor with SELECT count(*), wait_event_type FROM pg_stat_activity GROUP BY wait_event_type. A surge in Client wait events means PgBouncer's pool is exhausted — raise default_pool_size or shard across multiple PgBouncer instances. Never raise max_connections as a first response; you'll just shift the OOM problem to the OS.
bytes.code_snippet:
[databases]
mydb = host=127.0.0.1 port=5432 dbname=mydb

[pgbouncer]
pool_mode = transaction
max_client_conn = 500
default_pool_size = 10
reserve_pool_size = 2
reserve_pool_timeout = 3
server_idle_timeout = 30
listen_port = 6432
auth_type = scram-sha-256

bytes.topic: Security — encryption at rest/transit, row-level security, least privilege
bytes.title: PostgreSQL Security — RLS, Least Privilege, and Encryption You Actually Need to Configure
bytes.body: PostgreSQL doesn't encrypt at rest by default — that's delegated to the filesystem (e.g., LUKS on Linux or Azure Disk Encryption). What you control directly is transit encryption and access scoping. Force TLS by setting `ssl = on` in `postgresql.conf` and `hostssl all all 0.0.0.0/0 scram-sha-256` in `pg_hba.conf` — reject plain `host` entries entirely. For row-level security, enable it per table with `ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`, then create policies using `USING` (read filter) and `WITH CHECK` (write filter) clauses tied to `current_user` or a session variable like `app.current_tenant_id`. Never run application queries as a superuser. Create a dedicated role with only `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE` on required tables — no `DELETE`, no schema ownership. Use `SET ROLE` at session start if you need privilege escalation scoped to a transaction. Column-level encryption for PII fields can be layered with `pgcrypto`'s `pgp_sym_encrypt` before writes — decryption happens in the query, not the application layer.

bytes.topic: Migrations — zero-downtime migrations, backward compatibility, tooling
bytes.title: PostgreSQL Zero-Downtime Migrations — NOT VALID, Backfill, Then Enforce
bytes.body:
Adding a NOT NULL column to a large PostgreSQL table the naive way — `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN col TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'x'` — acquires an `AccessExclusiveLock` and rewrites the table on PostgreSQL < 11. Even on PG 11+, where constant defaults are stored in catalog metadata instead of rewriting, the NOT NULL check still scans every row under a lock.

The safe pattern splits the migration into three independent transactions: first add the column as nullable (near-instant, no lock contention), then backfill existing rows in bounded batches using `UPDATE ... WHERE id BETWEEN $1 AND $2` to avoid long-running transactions bloating `pg_wal`, and finally enforce the constraint with `ADD CONSTRAINT col_not_null CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID` followed separately by `VALIDATE CONSTRAINT col_not_null`.

`NOT VALID` skips scanning existing rows entirely — zero lock. `VALIDATE CONSTRAINT` runs under `ShareUpdateExclusiveLock`, which blocks DDL but allows concurrent reads and writes. This is the only PostgreSQL-native path to constraint enforcement without a table freeze. Tools like `pgroll` automate this three-phase pattern and manage dual-schema views for backward compatibility during rollouts.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — slow queries, connection counts, replication lag, storage growth
bytes.title: postgresql_de Monitoring — Slow Queries, Connections, Replication Lag & Storage in One View
bytes.body: PostgreSQL exposes everything you need through its system catalog views — no external agent required for baseline observability. For slow queries, `pg_stat_statements` is your first stop: sort by `total_exec_time / calls` to surface high-average-latency queries, or by `total_exec_time` to find cumulative cost leaders. Reset it with `pg_stat_statements_reset()` after schema changes to avoid stale baselines. Connection pressure lives in `pg_stat_activity` — group by `state` to distinguish active, idle, and idle-in-transaction sessions. Idle-in-transaction connections are particularly dangerous; they hold locks and block autovacuum. Replication lag is exposed via `pg_stat_replication`: the `write_lag`, `flush_lag`, and `replay_lag` interval columns give you per-standby pipeline depth. A non-null `sent_lsn - replay_lsn` delta quantifies byte lag precisely. For storage growth, `pg_database_size(current_database())` gives total DB size, while `pg_total_relation_size(relid)` from `pg_stat_user_tables` ranks bloated tables including their TOAST and indexes. Combine these into a single monitoring query and schedule it via `pg_cron` or scrape it with a Prometheus `postgres_exporter` custom collector.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- PostgreSQL unified monitoring snapshot
SELECT 'slow_queries' AS metric,
       query,
       round((total_exec_time / calls)::numeric, 2) AS avg_ms,
       calls
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY avg_ms DESC
LIMIT 5

UNION ALL

SELECT 'idle_in_txn_connections',
       usename || '@' || datname,
       EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now() - state_change))::numeric,
       NULL
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'idle in transaction'

UNION ALL

SELECT 'replication_lag_bytes',
       application_name,
       (sent_lsn - replay_lsn)::numeric,
       NULL
FROM pg_stat_replication;
